ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and non-trivial extension of prior DEPLAMBDA-style semantics: UDEPLAMBDA introduces a graph-capable variable-binding mechanism (BIND) and an enhancement step to map UD graphs to logical forms in an (almost) language-independent way, which clearly differentiates it from earlier tree-only, lexicalized approaches. From a theory-first perspective this is an informative advance because it directly addresses long-distance dependencies and cross-lingual applicability using only UD labels and POS, but it stops short of full lexicalization and formal guarantees for compositional correctness, so I view it as notable novelty rather than a radically new paradigm. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful novelty: an (almost) language-independent semantic interface that maps Universal Dependencies into logical forms and, importantly, a graph-capable variable-binding mechanism (BIND) to handle long-distance dependencies and dependency graphs—something prior DEPLAMBDA-style approaches could not do. Its emphasis on unlexicalized UD information and an enhancement step for cross-lingual applicability, together with empirical multilingual QA evaluation (English, Spanish, German) and creation of translated datasets, clearly differentiates it from prior work and demonstrates non-trivial technique extensions. While this is not a radical reconceptualization of semantic parsing, it meaningfully advances UD-based, language-agnostic composition, so would inform experts working on multilingual semantic interfaces.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and non-trivial extension of prior DEPLAMBDA-style semantics: UDEPLAMBDA introduces a graph-capable variable-binding mechanism (BIND) and an enhancement step to map UD graphs to logical forms in an (almost) language-independent way, which clearly differentiates it from earlier tree-only, lexicalized approaches. From a theory-first perspective this is an informative advance because it directly addresses long-distance dependencies and cross-lingual applicability using only UD labels and POS, but it stops short of full lexicalization and formal guarantees for compositional correctness, so I view it as notable novelty rather than a radically new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful novelty: an (almost) language-independent semantic interface that maps Universal Dependencies into logical forms and, importantly, a graph-capable variable-binding mechanism (BIND) to handle long-distance dependencies and dependency graphs—something prior DEPLAMBDA-style approaches could not do. Its emphasis on unlexicalized UD information and an enhancement step for cross-lingual applicability, together with empirical multilingual QA evaluation (English, Spanish, German) and creation of translated datasets, clearly differentiates it from prior work and demonstrates non-trivial technique extensions. While this is not a radical reconceptualization of semantic parsing, it meaningfully advances UD-based, language-agnostic composition, so would inform experts working on multilingual semantic interfaces.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): UDEPLAMBDA tackles an important problem—providing a nearly language-independent mapping from Universal Dependencies to logical forms and extending composition to dependency graphs via an explicit BIND variable‑binding mechanism—an advance that meaningfully broadens cross‑lingual semantic parsing. Its evaluation across English, Spanish, and German and state‑of‑the‑art performance on GraphQuestions suggest researchers will take its approach seriously. From my theoretical standpoint, the work is less than transformative because it trades off lexicalized semantic specificity (and leaves quantifier handling unimplemented) and does not provide formal guarantees of compositional correctness; nevertheless it opens significant practical and research directions for UD‑based, multilingual semantic interfaces. | B(4): This paper tackles an important problem—language-independent semantic parsing—by providing a UD-based, unlexicalized interface (UDEPLAMBDA) with a novel BIND mechanism that handles graphs and long-distance dependencies; those design choices align well with my emphasis on UD-based, language-agnostic approaches. Empirically it is convincing: evaluation on three typologically distinct languages (English, Spanish, German) and wins over strong baselines, including the best reported result on GraphQuestions, indicate practical cross-lingual utility. Limitations (loss of lexical specificity, missing quantifier/negation handling) reduce immediate universality but do not negate the method’s value as a portable semantic interface and a clear springboard for future work.

reviewer_a.justification: UDEPLAMBDA tackles an important problem—providing a nearly language-independent mapping from Universal Dependencies to logical forms and extending composition to dependency graphs via an explicit BIND variable‑binding mechanism—an advance that meaningfully broadens cross‑lingual semantic parsing. Its evaluation across English, Spanish, and German and state‑of‑the‑art performance on GraphQuestions suggest researchers will take its approach seriously. From my theoretical standpoint, the work is less than transformative because it trades off lexicalized semantic specificity (and leaves quantifier handling unimplemented) and does not provide formal guarantees of compositional correctness; nevertheless it opens significant practical and research directions for UD‑based, multilingual semantic interfaces.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important problem—language-independent semantic parsing—by providing a UD-based, unlexicalized interface (UDEPLAMBDA) with a novel BIND mechanism that handles graphs and long-distance dependencies; those design choices align well with my emphasis on UD-based, language-agnostic approaches. Empirically it is convincing: evaluation on three typologically distinct languages (English, Spanish, German) and wins over strong baselines, including the best reported result on GraphQuestions, indicate practical cross-lingual utility. Limitations (loss of lexical specificity, missing quantifier/negation handling) reduce immediate universality but do not negate the method’s value as a portable semantic interface and a clear springboard for future work.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core ACL/EMNLP/NeurIPS topic—multilingual semantic parsing and an interface between Universal Dependencies and logical forms—by proposing a novel graph-capable variable-binding mechanism (BIND) and an (almost) language-independent composition procedure. The work targets the right audience, combining linguistic/formal-semantic concerns with empirical evaluation across English, Spanish, and German and competitive baselines on established QA datasets. From my theory-first perspective I note the importance of a formal specification or proofs of compositional correctness (which I would expect in a full submission), but those methodological desiderata do not change that the topic, scope, and positioning are an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it tackles multilingual semantic parsing using a language-agnostic UD-based interface (UDEPLAMBDA) and a novel BIND mechanism, which aligns with my emphasis on universal syntactic representations. The authors evaluate across three typologically distinct languages (English, Spanish, German) on realistic KBQA benchmarks (WebQuestions, GraphQuestions) and compare to multiple strong baselines, including DEPLAMBDA and CCG-based methods, demonstrating clear downstream improvements. Minor gaps (e.g., no mention of code release or hyperparameter reporting, and some semantic phenomena left unimplemented) do not undermine its relevance or audience fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core ACL/EMNLP/NeurIPS topic—multilingual semantic parsing and an interface between Universal Dependencies and logical forms—by proposing a novel graph-capable variable-binding mechanism (BIND) and an (almost) language-independent composition procedure. The work targets the right audience, combining linguistic/formal-semantic concerns with empirical evaluation across English, Spanish, and German and competitive baselines on established QA datasets. From my theory-first perspective I note the importance of a formal specification or proofs of compositional correctness (which I would expect in a full submission), but those methodological desiderata do not change that the topic, scope, and positioning are an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it tackles multilingual semantic parsing using a language-agnostic UD-based interface (UDEPLAMBDA) and a novel BIND mechanism, which aligns with my emphasis on universal syntactic representations. The authors evaluate across three typologically distinct languages (English, Spanish, German) on realistic KBQA benchmarks (WebQuestions, GraphQuestions) and compare to multiple strong baselines, including DEPLAMBDA and CCG-based methods, demonstrating clear downstream improvements. Minor gaps (e.g., no mention of code release or hyperparameter reporting, and some semantic phenomena left unimplemented) do not undermine its relevance or audience fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, the BIND mechanism, and the enhancement step for UD) are stated early and followed by method and experiments, matching the intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and examples are mostly clear and the writing quality is good with thorough related-work coverage, but the presentation would benefit from a more formal, self-contained specification of the variable-binding mechanism and compositional operations (quantifier handling is deferred to supplementary material), which slightly reduces clarity for readers seeking a rigorous semantic account. | B(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, BIND mechanism, UD-based enhancement for cross-lingual parsing) are stated clearly up front, and the writing quality is reported as good. The methodology and multilingual experimental setup (English, Spanish, German; WebQuestions and GraphQuestions) are described so readers can follow the approach and its claimed advantages; limitations are explicitly discussed. One minor presentation gap in the summary is that evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and the presence of figures/tables are not specified, which suggests some sections could provide more detail for reproducibility and clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, the BIND mechanism, and the enhancement step for UD) are stated early and followed by method and experiments, matching the intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and examples are mostly clear and the writing quality is good with thorough related-work coverage, but the presentation would benefit from a more formal, self-contained specification of the variable-binding mechanism and compositional operations (quantifier handling is deferred to supplementary material), which slightly reduces clarity for readers seeking a rigorous semantic account.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, BIND mechanism, UD-based enhancement for cross-lingual parsing) are stated clearly up front, and the writing quality is reported as good. The methodology and multilingual experimental setup (English, Spanish, German; WebQuestions and GraphQuestions) are described so readers can follow the approach and its claimed advantages; limitations are explicitly discussed. One minor presentation gap in the summary is that evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and the presence of figures/tables are not specified, which suggests some sections could provide more detail for reproducibility and clarity.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, multiple GRAPHPARSER variants and prior work such as Reddy et al. 2014) and evaluates across multiple datasets and language translations, which supports substantive empirical comparison. However the summary omits key experimental details (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and code availability not reported) making it hard to fully assess fairness and reproducibility; this prevents a top score. The authors thoroughly situate their method relative to competing approaches and analyze architectural differences (e.g., BIND, enhancement step), but the paper does not clearly report whether and when prior methods perform comparably, nor provide full conditions to confirm exact parity of evaluation, so I place this at 4/5. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of strong baselines (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, three GRAPHPARSER variants and prior work such as Reddy et al. 2014) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (WebQuestions, GraphQuestions) plus German/Spanish translations, which aligns well with my emphasis on multilingual empirical evaluation. Comparisons appear to be across the same datasets and include graph-capable competitors, but the summary omits key experimental details (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters/code not reported) and the use of author-created translations could raise reproducibility/fairness questions. Overall the empirical comparison is strong and relevant, with only minor omissions that prevent it being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, multiple GRAPHPARSER variants and prior work such as Reddy et al. 2014) and evaluates across multiple datasets and language translations, which supports substantive empirical comparison. However the summary omits key experimental details (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and code availability not reported) making it hard to fully assess fairness and reproducibility; this prevents a top score. The authors thoroughly situate their method relative to competing approaches and analyze architectural differences (e.g., BIND, enhancement step), but the paper does not clearly report whether and when prior methods perform comparably, nor provide full conditions to confirm exact parity of evaluation, so I place this at 4/5.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of strong baselines (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, three GRAPHPARSER variants and prior work such as Reddy et al. 2014) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (WebQuestions, GraphQuestions) plus German/Spanish translations, which aligns well with my emphasis on multilingual empirical evaluation. Comparisons appear to be across the same datasets and include graph-capable competitors, but the summary omits key experimental details (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters/code not reported) and the use of author-created translations could raise reproducibility/fairness questions. Overall the empirical comparison is strong and relevant, with only minor omissions that prevent it being fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a clear, testable claim and provides multilingual empirical evidence showing improvements, but lacks formal guarantees and fuller methodological reporting (metrics/statistics, hyperparameters, code) and does not implement some important semantic phenomena. Given the solid empirical gains and plausible technical approach, but acknowledging the methodological and formal gaps that prevent a higher rating, a score of 4 balances the paper’s strengths and the noted deficiencies.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (a UD-based, graph-capable semantic interface with a BIND mechanism) and provides cross-lingual experimental evidence showing improved QA performance, so empirical support exists. However, from the summary there is no formal specification or proof of compositional correctness for the substitution/composition scheme or the variable-binding mechanism, and the enhancement step appears heuristic and dataset-specific without formal coverage analysis — both are deficiencies I penalize. Important semantic phenomena (quantifiers, negation, intentional verbs) are acknowledged but not implemented, and experimental details (metrics/statistical tests, hyperparameters, code) are not reported, leaving methodological gaps. Overall the contribution is promising empirically but lacks the formal guarantees and deeper analysis I require for a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes clear, testable claims and supports them with multilingual experiments (English, Spanish, German) showing improvements over several strong baselines and a new best on GraphQuestions, which aligns with my preference for cross-lingual evaluation. The technical approach (UD-only composition, an enhancement step, and the BIND mechanism for graphs) is plausible and described in sufficient detail; there are no evident mathematical errors in the summary. I note methodological gaps: evaluation metrics/statistical testing and hyperparameter reporting are missing in the summary, and the created translations/enhancement step could introduce language-specific artifacts that are not ablated. These are minor but relevant concerns that do not overturn the main empirical claims, so I rate it 4/5.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers strong multilingual empirical results and meaningful gains on benchmarks, but falls short on formalization, ablation/sensitivity analyses, and reproducibility details (hyperparameters/code). Given the clear empirical strengths and thorough related work coverage weighed against these important but addressable gaps, a score of 4 reflects a solid contribution that would be strengthened by additional analysis and release of experimental details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a convincing multilingual empirical evaluation (English, Spanish, German) and addresses graph-structured dependencies with a novel BIND mechanism, which supports the main empirical claims. However, from a theory-first perspective it fails to provide formal specification or compositional correctness proofs for the variable-binding/BIND mechanism and relies on an enhancement step whose coverage and generality are not formally analyzed. Ablation/sensitivity analyses and experimental details (metrics, hyperparameters, code) appear missing, though related work is well-covered and several limitations are acknowledged. Given solid multilingual experiments but important gaps in formalization, ablations, and reproducibility, the assessment is moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong on multilingual grounds (English, Spanish, German) and compare against multiple relevant baselines, even reporting best-known results on GraphQuestions, which aligns with my emphasis on typologically diverse evaluation and downstream QA. However, the summary lacks mention of ablation/sensitivity analyses and hyperparameter/code release, limiting assessment of which components (e.g., the BIND mechanism or enhancement step) drive gains and hurting reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper discusses concrete limitations and unhandled constructions, which I view positively. Overall this is a solid empirical study with minor but important gaps in ablations and reproducibility details.

main_claims: Introduce UDEPLAMBDA, a semantic interface for Universal Dependencies enabling an (almost) language-independent mapping from UD to logical forms.
Design a graph-capable variable-binding mechanism (BIND) to handle long-distance dependencies and dependency graphs, unlike DEPLAMBDA which cannot handle graph-structured input.
Rely on unlexicalized UD information (dependency labels and POS) and an enhancement step to enrich UD trees before composition, allowing cross-lingual semantic parsing.

Empirically evaluate UDEPLAMBDA on question answering against Freebase in English, Spanish, and German, reporting that it outperforms strong baselines across languages and datasets and achieves the strongest result to date on GraphQuestions for English.
_summary.methodology: UDEPLAMBDA restricts binarization, substitution, and composition to information encoded in Universal Dependencies, uses an enhancement step to add missing syntactic and long-distance dependencies, and employs a variable-binding mechanism (BIND) with unlexicalized lambda-expression substitutions to produce logical forms from dependency graphs.
datasets: German and Spanish translations of WebQuestions and GraphQuestions (created for this work)
baselines: three versions of GRAPHPARSER (entity cliques, dependency trees, CCG-based semantic derivations)
prior work baselines referenced (e.g., Reddy et al. 2014)
limitations: To achieve language independence UDEPLAMBDA sacrifices semantic specificity; it does not lexicalize substitutions so cannot canonicalize relations that depend on lexical context (e.g., distinguishing arg1 vs arg2 for broke with window).

Constructions that require lexical information (quantifiers like every/some/most, negation markers like no/not, intentional verbs like believe/said) are not handled specially by UD and are not currently implemented in UDEPLAMBDA.
Quantifier handling is discussed but not implemented (in supplementary material).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - multilingual semantic parsing / semantic interface for Universal Dependencies

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Semanticist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in formal semantics and computational linguistics with a track record of work on compositional semantics and formal language-theoretic guarantees.
traits: penalizes papers that do not provide a formal specification or proof of compositional correctness for their semantics (deducts points if none provided)
penalizes reliance on heuristic or dataset-specific enhancement steps without formal justification or analysis of coverage
rewards explicit formal treatment of variable-binding and scoping (adds points if rigorous definitions and formal properties are given)
expects error analysis of semantic failures tied to linguistic phenomena (deducts points if missing or superficial)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes formal rigor and clear theoretical contribution; will downweight empirical gains unless accompanied by formal guarantees or deep analysis of why the method works.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Multilingual Evaluator
reviewer_b.background: Researcher focused on multilingual NLP and semantic parsing for downstream QA/search applications, with experience building and evaluating cross-lingual systems against knowledge bases.
traits: rewards comprehensive multilingual empirical evaluation (adds points if ≥3 typologically distinct languages evaluated)
rewards methods that are practically language-agnostic and rely on universal syntactic representations (adds points for UD-based, unlexicalized approaches)
rewards clear improvements on established downstream tasks/datasets (adds points for state-of-the-art or strong wins on benchmarks like GraphQuestions)
penalizes lack of comparison to strong baselines or missing ablations for components that enable cross-lingual transfer (deducts points if baselines/ablations absent)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on practical impact and empirical evidence of cross-lingual generality; will favor methods that demonstrably improve downstream multilingual QA and are easy to port across languages.
